intro.description: The capstone review. A passage may hold anywhere from zero to many fallacies. Read all three paragraphs, then mark only what is genuinely faulty.

sentences.text: A research team studied whether a new tutoring program improved reading scores.
They enrolled students from six schools chosen to reflect the district.
Half were assigned to the program and half to the usual curriculum at random.
Scores were measured before the program began and again at the end of the year.
The team wrote down what they expected to find before looking at the results.
On average, the tutored group gained a few points more than the control group.
The gain was smaller in the two largest schools.
The authors report the size of the effect and how uncertain it is.
They note that attendance varied and may have influenced the outcome.
All of the anonymized data was published for others to check.
The team recommends a second trial in a different district before scaling up.
They list the questions the current study could not answer.
Funding for any expansion would be released in stages tied to results.
If the follow-up shows no gain, the program will be discontinued.
The report frames its conclusion as promising but not yet settled.

hints.text: Strong, hedged evidence is not a fallacy — check whether any claim overreaches.
Each paragraph states method, limits, or next steps. There may be nothing to mark.
This study reasons carefully and contains no fallacy. Marking nothing may be correct.

passages.title: A Memo for the New Tool

sentences.text: Management circulated a memo urging every team to adopt the new workflow tool.
This bold, visionary change will leave only stubborn dinosaurs behind.
Teams that already use the tool report finishing their sprints on time.
Offices that switched tools last year also saw morale rise, so the tool clearly lifts morale.
A trial period is available for teams that want to test it first.
Some engineers worry about the time it will take to migrate.
When one manager raised that concern, a colleague shot back that he had complained about the last tool too, so why listen to him now.
The vendor offers training sessions throughout the transition.
IT has documented the migration steps for each team.
A rollback plan exists in case the switch causes problems.
At the meeting, one lead pleaded that her team had endured such a brutal quarter that the budget simply must be approved.
The finance office asked for the numbers behind each request.
A decision is expected after the next planning review.
Teams can submit written feedback until Friday.
The final rollout schedule will be shared once the feedback is reviewed.

expected.rationale: Charged words ('visionary,' 'stubborn dinosaurs') do the persuading instead of reasons.
Morale rising alongside the switch is treated as proof the tool caused it.
It deflects the concern by pointing at the manager's past complaints instead of answering it.
It argues for the budget from a hard quarter rather than the request's merits.

hints.text: Look for Loaded wording, a Causation slip, a Whataboutism deflection, and a Pity plea.
Four sentences lean on rhetoric instead of reasons; the rest describe process.
'visionary... dinosaurs' = Loaded Language; 'morale rose, so the tool lifts morale' = Correlation/Causation; 'you complained before' = Tu Quoque; 'such a brutal quarter' = Appeal to Pity.

sentences.text: The city is debating whether to fund the new downtown stadium.
Ever since the team announced the plan, downtown sales rose, so the stadium is already boosting the economy.
The mayor's office released a cost estimate for public review.
Anyone against it is just a bitter cynic who hates progress.
An independent firm was hired to check the projections.
Supporters held a rally downtown last weekend.
Either we build the stadium now or the city stays stuck in the past forever.
Planners say several sites are still being compared.
Polls in two other cities favored their stadiums, so ours is sure to be loved too.
A public hearing is scheduled for next month.
Most of the popular local accounts are sharing the plan, so it must be the right move.
Reject the funding and watch the whole downtown collapse into ruin.
Critics have asked for a detailed traffic and noise study.
The council will review the consultant's findings before voting.
Both sides expect that report to weigh heavily on the outcome.

expected.rationale: A rise in sales after the announcement is treated as proof the stadium caused it.
It labels opponents 'bitter cynics' instead of answering their objections.
It offers only 'build now' or 'stuck forever,' ignoring other options the piece itself mentions.
Two other cities are used to conclude ours will surely be loved too.
Popularity among online accounts is treated as proof the plan is right.
It pushes against rejection with a frightening image of collapse instead of evidence.

hints.text: Several moves here: a Causation slip, a Personal attack, a Distorted choice, a Hasty rule, a Popularity appeal, and a Scare.
Six sentences are faulty; the rest are neutral reports of process.

'sales rose, so boosting' = Post Hoc; 'bitter cynic' = Ad Hominem; 'build now or stuck forever' = False Dilemma; 'two cities, so ours too' = Hasty Generalization; 'popular accounts sharing' = Bandwagon; 'reject and downtown collapses' = Appeal to Fear.
